tweets.text:
I think most AI trading tools sound smarter than they are.

But I keep searching (heh).
Already tested @minara and @SurfAI.

Yesterday, decoded to try the new tool from @StableJack_xyz.

I'd rate it 7.3/10.
Main use case: Analyze and trade from the same place.

The agent gives a pretty decent breakdown of the bull/bear case, and the key levels to watch.

Then you can go straight to the trading terminal, check more market sentiment and decide if the setup makes sense.

I’ll be doing a deeper dive on this soon (+other trading agents), so keep an eye out.

tweets.text:
The role of stablecoins changed over time.

They started as a way for crypto traders to avoid crypto volatility without leaving crypto. That was the main loop for years: buy USDT, sit in stables, move funds to another exchange or wallet, then rotate back into the next trade.

They were super useful in DeFi. But that's changed in recent times.

Stablecoins are being used more and more in sectors that most of us wouldn't connect to crypto, like:

• Payroll
• Stock settlement
• AI agent payments

They're clearly moving beyond trading and becoming part of how apps and businesses move money.

I'll use USD1 as an example ↓

1. Payroll

@Zebec_HQ is paying 65K+ workers globally through USD1.

They likely opted for this because the rails are faster and cheaper than what the banks are offering.

The big advantage here is speed, as workers get their salaries instantly.

This makes payroll less dependent on bank hours, transfer windows, and slow cross-border rails.

2. Stock settlement

Binance recently added USD1 as a settlement asset for stock trading.

I think people are underestimating how important this is.

Having the majority of this stablecoin's circulating supply on Binance (~87%), and now routing stock settlement through it, creates a powerful flywheel, where:

• Traders need USD1 to enter/exit positions
• Market makers need USD1 to provide liquidity
• Binance has more reason to expand USD1 across more products

As one of the first real tests for equities settling through stablecoins, this could become one of USD1’s biggest growth levers.

3. AI agent payments

@worldlibertyfi shipped AgentPay earlier this year, which lets AI agents move money within preset spending rules.

An agent can pay for APIs, compute, data, or services without needing a human to approve every small transaction.

This is a sector where stablecoins make the most sense to me.

Agents need money that is stable, programmable, always online, and capable of settling in seconds. USD1 is used inside that payment flow, where the whole loop runs without manual approval.

It essentially becomes working capital for any piece of software that needs to spend money on its own.

My take

The stablecoin race is no longer just about listings or liquidity. It’s about getting embedded into flows where money moves frequently:

Payroll, stock settlement, agents, perps margining, prediction markets.
You get the idea.

Once a stablecoin becomes part of how an app or business operates, replacing it can be a hassle. That's why first movers will end up with an edge.

For the record, I'm personally holding a portion of my stablecoin in USD1 and putting it to work across different venues.

Adoption over everything.

Disclosure: I'm a USD1 holder myself.

tweets.text:
I got a few frens↓

• @Defi_Warhol – best for crypto neobank comparisons, card perks, and cashback breakdowns

• @strato_money – original takes on stablecoin neobanks and card rewards

• @sytaylor – solid fintech creator covering neobanks, payments, and banking infra

• @chingtsengtw – frameworks on what crypto cards compete on beyond cashback

• @KoppKnows – original takes on full-stack neobanks, yield, and borrowing

• @0xshawnpang – builder POV on stablecoin payments and onchain finance

• @randhindi – founder/operator with smart takes on product design

• @izqui9 – operator lens on card positioning and tier lists

• @ravi_riley – writes about product strategy and why cards alone aren't enough

• @0xVishnya – smaller account, does cashback and category comparisons

Who else should I include?

tweets.text: One reason it's more difficult to work with people you don't have faith in is simply that there are more possibilities to consider whenever they do something. You also have to consider all the ways they might have screwed up. Even if they didn't, you still have to think about it.

thread_tweets.text: One reason it's more difficult to work with people you don't have faith in is simply that there are more possibilities to consider whenever they do something. You also have to consider all the ways they might have screwed up. Even if they didn't, you still have to think about it.
thread_tweets.full_text: One reason it's more difficult to work with people you don't have faith in is simply that there are more possibilities to consider whenever they do something. You also have to consider all the ways they might have screwed up. Even if they didn't, you still have to think about it.

thread_tweets.text: For example, if you ask someone you trust for a number and get a surprising answer, you'll have to consider multiple possible explanations. But if it's from someone you don't trust, in addition to all those you have to consider all the ways they might have got the number wrong.
thread_tweets.full_text: For example, if you ask someone you trust for a number and get a surprising answer, you'll have to consider multiple possible explanations. But if it's from someone you don't trust, in addition to all those you have to consider all the ways they might have got the number wrong.

tweets.full_text:
One reason it's more difficult to work with people you don't have faith in is simply that there are more possibilities to consider whenever they do something. You also have to consider all the ways they might have screwed up. Even if they didn't, you still have to think about it.

For example, if you ask someone you trust for a number and get a surprising answer, you'll have to consider multiple possible explanations. But if it's from someone you don't trust, in addition to all those you have to consider all the ways they might have got the number wrong.

tweets.text:
The market right now reminds me of someone who's been in a toxic relationship for so long that they can't recognize when something good is actually happening.

Iran deal signed, CLARITY Act moving forward, stablecoin adoption at ATH, real onchain businesses like Hyperliquid, Polymarket and Collector printing revenue and getting mainstream attention.

However, this market has been beaten down so badly that it can't even recognize good news anymore. Every bullish catalyst gets dismissed, every positive headline gets met with "yeah but..."

This is what capitulation looks like. Just complete emotional exhaustion where even the most obvious bullish catalysts get ignored because there is no belief left.

That's the most bullish signal there is.

Markets don't bottom on good news. They bottom when good news stops mattering. 

And that's exactly where we are right now.

So excited to see this all over my timeline:

Just as TCGs are seeing the highest demand in history, @vibes_tcg dropped their newest set, now available in one of the biggest retailers across the US.

This is going to be a massive catalyst in bringing @pudgypenguins and @moonbirds to the mainstream, growing the community and demand for both IPs exponentially.

Had so much fun collecting the first two sets. Time to rip some packs again.

tweets.text:
Recap of the crypto market over the past 2 weeks:

- $BTC dumped from $74k to $60k in one week after the 32 BTC sale by Strategy. Increasing FUD around MSTR has lead $STRC to depeg to $83 at the bottom.

- The main perp DEXes have been doing well: $HYPE reached a new ATH at $77 and $LIT is up more than 2x in a month

- $ZEC had a -60% crash in a day after the reveal of a potential vulnerability. It bounced 2x from there.

- $WLD has been the strongest coin in June, up almost 2x. OpenAI IPO proxy + tokenomics upgrade in July

- $JTO remains very strong with the JTX launch as a catalyst.

- $LAB and $BEAT were the crime coins of June, and respectively reached $25bn and $11bn FDV.

- $TAO and $VVV pump and dumped on the Fable ban news

- $XPL ran almost 2x on the back of the Plasma One launch

- $EIGEN is pumping because of its successful pivot to AI

tweets.text:
If this is true, then the BTC/SPX chart "fairly priced" should be even lower, which would mean 6 years (or even more) without any outperformance from BTC vs SPX, and at this point one can ask if BTC does have any chance at become a SoV.
If it's not a SoV, it's not strictly 'worthless', but at least it's not worthy of interest as a long-term investment.

tweets.text:
In trading, fuck-ups are 95% about psychology and not technical skills

One of the things you can derive from that is that whenever your account is in a precarious position, you should ask a (trader) friend what to do, because his perspective will have zero bias compared to you

I think at least 80% of the time you’re just holding onto a position that you should close

thread_tweets.text: No, because AI replaces intelligence, that only thing that made humans really unique. It's not about automating a skill, but replacing anything a human can do. The Internet led to new opportunities, AI will be able to do those opportunities itself. There will be many one person companies, but not a lot of companies that will hire people.

thread_tweets.text: I think the US will invest in the whole AI production chain, not just the LLMs. At this point only the US and China have a full AI stack, I don't see other countries developing them anytime soon, especially not frontier models. That means the RoW will likely use US and Chinese models, not local ones. Hard to predict now how this will play out of course, but my AI investments are in the whole stack, and both in China and the US.

tweets.text:
If you make it to 30 and you aren’t cynical about the way the world works, you are a sheep.

If you are 40 are can retain your optimism in the face of all that, you are a champ.

I’d like to imagine that once you’re 75 and you can stare pessimism & death in the face & laugh, you are an absolute effing warrior. 

We should all aspire to that.
